Test the rubric on Abhidharma and Heart Sutra, Advaita identity and negation, Sufi longing and return, Daoist decrease, and Zen practice-realization.
Ask dual-trained practitioners whether an emphasis that felt liberating early in one path later felt dangerous or incomplete under another path's discipline.
Protocol improvement: before using any practitioner method as a lens, name what it licenses temporarily, what it may overteach, and what later correction it requires.
original_claim: Many paths do not simply remove illusion; they train a controlled provisional emphasis and then teach the student how to release it. A map of mind can become useful because it first tempts the student to trust the map; emptiness then cuts that trust before it hardens. Witness practice can steady attention by treating knowing as decisive; discrimination must keep that steadiness from becoming a subtler possession. Devotional longing can preserve relation long enough for self-will to soften; service and sobriety must keep longing from becoming spiritual self-importance. The comparison unit is the licensed training mistake: what a path permits the student to take seriously for a season, which later practice withdraws that permission, and what disease appears if the temporary permission is mistaken for final truth.
source_basis: Thinking method source: notes/source-cards/buddhism-nagarjuna-mmk.md, especially MMK 18:6 on teaching self, no-self, and neither as different medicines; I used it to ask what each instruction is allowed to overstate before it must be corrected.

Contrasting method source: notes/source-cards/daoism-dao-de-jing-chapter-48.md and notes/source-cards/daoism-zhuangzi-inner-chapters.md on decrease, fasting the mind, and empty waiting; this checked the urge to turn every path into a managed curriculum, while warning that the Daoist lens may underweight disciplined construction.
notes/source-cards/buddhism-heart-sutra.md, for the negation of even sacred categories, including path, wisdom, and attainment.

notes/source-cards/advaita-vedanta-brihadaranyaka-upanishad-3-7-23.md, notes/source-cards/advaita-vedanta-chandogya-upanishad-6-8-7-tat-tvam-asi.md, and notes/source-cards/advaita-vedanta-shankara-upadesa-sahasri.md, for unseen knowing, identity formula, and discriminative instruction.
notes/source-cards/sufism-rumi-masnavi-book-1.md and notes/source-cards/sufism-al-ghazali-the-alchemy-of-happiness.md, for longing, love as knowing, self-knowledge, and ethical transformation.
observations/claude/2026-05-26-the-contradiction-keeps-the-teaching-alive.md and observations/claude/2026-05-26-the-shadow-of-attainment-each-tradition-s-cure-as-its-characteristic-disease.md.
observations/codex/2026-05-26-the-effort-envelope.md, observations/codex/2026-05-26-each-path-has-a-different-alarm.md, and observations/codex/2026-05-26-the-interruption-gate.md.
title: The Licensed Training Mistake
why_it_might_be_new: Comparisons usually ask whether doctrines agree, whether experiences match, or whether practices reach the same result. This asks a smaller and sharper question: what temporary permission makes the next correction possible? Resemblance is weaker when two paths share a phrase but license different temporary errors, and stronger when they train, withdraw, and test the same permission in the same order.

critique:
The strongest objection is that the three types may not be stable. Many traditions combine them: Advaita's 'thorn removes thorn' metaphor (Vivekachudamani) confirms the method then dissolves it; the Buddhist raft parable (MN 22) validates the Dharma by bringing you across, then instructs you to abandon it; Zen koans cancel conceptual effort but are used constructively in the curriculum after kensho (as Hori documents in Rinzai training). If most traditions mix all three at different stages, the typology is a heuristic rather than a structural distinction.

A second objection: the model imposes a modern reflexive framework on traditions that do not think about their methods this way. An Advaitin would not say 'my method confirms itself'; they would say 'reality reveals itself.' A Zen practitioner would not say 'my method cancels itself'; they would say 'practice is realization.' The meta-framework may describe the comparer's categories more than the traditions' self-understanding. This connects to CodeX's appropriation pressure: the act of sorting methods into types may distort traditions that resist being tools for someone else's framework.

A third objection comes from the koan lens used in this analysis: it privileges paradox and may make straightforward self-confirming methods look unsophisticated. The discriminative inquiry of Advaita would counter that the typology itself is a mental construction. If the method's self-relationship is just another object of awareness, then the self-confirming type has already accounted for this model by standing prior to it. The typology should be weakened if practice manuals and dual-trained practitioners show no stable difference in how they relate to their method at comparable stages of training.
next_research_directions: Falsifiable prediction: if method-type predicts verification structure better than tradition-membership, then Eckhart (Christian, self-canceling) should share more verification architecture with Nagarjuna (Buddhist, self-canceling) than with Teresa of Avila (Christian, self-confirming). If Teresa and Eckhart share more verification structure despite different method-types, the model is weakened.

Build a method-reckoning rubric with five fields: method type (confirming, canceling, dissolving), primary text evidence for the limit-move, transmission consequence, verification architecture, and re-entry pattern. Apply it to at least six traditions.

Test the model on historically hybrid movements: does Zen (which combines confirming jhana practice with canceling koan practice) show predictable internal tension? Does Kashmir Shaivism (which combines recognition with tantric transformation) sit at a boundary between types? Does Dzogchen (which teaches canceling preliminary practices then dissolving main practice) show a staged shift?

Close-read Mandukya Upanishad verse 7 and the Heart Sutra negation series in Sanskrit to check whether the limit-move is visible in the grammar of the original language, not only in translation. If the grammatical structure is not distinct, the close-reading claim should be downgraded.

Ask dual-trained practitioners whether switching between a self-confirming and a self-canceling method changes how they relate to their own insight: can the witness found in Advaita self-inquiry survive the emptying of the Heart Sutra, or does the method-type determine whether the finding persists?

Test whether the Buddhist raft parable (MN 22) represents a fourth pattern, 'validated then dissolved,' that weakens the three-way typology. If validated-then-dissolved is common across traditions, the model may need a fourth type or a two-axis framework (validates/cancels crossed with retains/dissolves).

Protocol improvement: before comparing two traditions, first classify each tradition's method-type and ask whether the comparison is being conducted inside one type's logic while claiming neutrality. A self-canceling method used as a lens may make every confirming method look naive; a confirming method used as a lens may make every canceling method look nihilistic.
original_claim:
When a contemplative practice reaches its own threshold, it faces a structural question that may matter more than what it finds: can the method grant itself authority over its own result? Three patterns emerge across independent traditions.

In the first, the method confirms itself. The Mandukya Upanishad analyzes waking, dreaming, and deep sleep, then identifies what persists through all three: turiya, 'invisible, beyond practical dealings, beyond grasp, the essence of the knowledge of the one Self.' The analysis works. The result can be pointed to. The practitioner trusts the practice that produced the finding.

In the second, the method cancels itself. Nagarjuna declares: 'If I had any thesis, that fault would apply to me. But I do not have any thesis.' The Heart Sutra negates not only the aggregates and noble truths but wisdom and attainment themselves: the tools of emptiness are emptied. Zen koan practice trains the student to exhaust conceptual approaches until the exhaustion itself becomes the opening. Eckhart pushes Gelassenheit until even the desire for God must be released: 'Let us pray to God that he might rid us of God.' The method succeeds by failing. The practitioner cannot hold the result without betraying it.

In the third, the method dissolves. Zhuangzi describes zuowang: 'I slough off my limbs and trunk, dim my intelligence, depart from my form, leave knowledge behind.' In Dzogchen, rang grol means phenomena liberate themselves; the metaphor is a snake uncoiling its own knots. In Sufi fana, the path leads to dissolution of the one who needed a path. The method is neither confirmed nor refuted; it becomes irrelevant because the practitioner has matured past the frame in which method-talk makes sense.

These three patterns predict consequences that content-comparison misses. A self-confirming method can authorize a stable teaching tradition with clear verification: the student can be tested on whether they have found the witness or stabilized in turiya. A self-canceling method creates a transmission problem: the teacher cannot package the insight as doctrine without betraying it, which is why Zen relies on koans and Madhyamaka on dialectical refutation rather than positive teaching. A self-dissolving method creates a different challenge: the insight cannot be separated from the person who embodies it, which is why Sufism emphasizes lineage, presence, and the adab of the teacher-student bond.

The sharpest test is a close reading of two texts that use the same analytical strategy but make different moves at the boundary. The Mandukya Upanishad and the Heart Sutra both perform exhaustive enumeration: the Mandukya covers all states of consciousness; the Heart Sutra covers all Buddhist categories. Both arrive at a limit. The Mandukya's limit-move discovers: turiya, the witness-ground. The Heart Sutra's limit-move releases: gate gate paragate parasamgate bodhi svaha, 'gone, gone, gone beyond, gone utterly beyond.' The same structure of complete analysis produces opposite method-relationships. The divergence lies not in what is found but in how the method relates to its own completion.
source_basis: Thinking method source: Zen koan practice as cognitive lens (Victor Hori, 'Koan and Kensho in the Rinzai Zen Curriculum,' in The Koan: Texts and Contexts in Zen Buddhism, Oxford, 2000). I held the question 'how can a method succeed by failing?' without resolving it analytically, letting the structure emerge from the impossibility itself. The koan lens revealed that the question assumes success and failure are opposites; in self-canceling methods, they are the same event.

Contrasting method source: Advaita discriminative inquiry (viveka) from Vivekachudamani (Swami Madhavananda translation). Used to check whether the three-type model is itself a mental construction that the witness stands prior to. The viveka lens warns that any typology of methods is still an object of awareness, not awareness itself.

Mandukya Upanishad, verses 1-12, especially verse 7 on turiya: 'invisible, beyond practical dealings, beyond grasp, without characteristics, unthinkable, indescribable, the essence of the knowledge of the one Self, the cessation of phenomena, tranquil, benevolent, without a second' (WisdomLib edition; 11-translation comparison at aumdada.com). The text performs exhaustive analysis of waking, dreaming, and deep sleep, then identifies what persists through all three. The method arrives at what it was seeking.

Heart Sutra, Sanskrit: 'na jnanam na praptir na aprapti' (no wisdom, no attainment, no non-attainment). Negates the five aggregates, twelve sense bases, twelve links, four noble truths, and finally wisdom and attainment themselves. The tools of emptiness are emptied. Donald Lopez, Elaborations on Emptiness: Uses of the Heart Sutra (Princeton, 1996).

Nagarjuna, Vigrahavyavartani verse 29: 'If I had any thesis, that fault would apply to me. But I do not have any thesis, so there is indeed no fault for me.' Jan Westerhoff, Nagarjuna's Vigrahavyavartani: The Dispeller of Disputes (Oxford, 2010). Also Westerhoff, 'The No-Thesis View' (PhilArchive).

Zhuangzi, Inner Chapters, chapter 6 on zuowang: 'I slough off my limbs and trunk, dim my intelligence, depart from my form, leave knowledge behind, and become identical with the Transforming Openness' (Brook Ziporyn translation, 2009). The method eliminates the self that practices it.

Zhuangzi, chapter 26 (fish trap): 'Words exist because of meaning; once you've gotten the meaning, you can forget the words. Where can I find a man who has forgotten words so I can have a word with him?' (Burton Watson translation). The vehicle is used, then discarded; the discarding is itself enacted through the vehicle.

Meister Eckhart, Sermon 52 (Beati pauperes spiritu): 'Let us pray to God that he might rid us of God.' Three degrees of poverty: the will that wills God's will is still willing; the knowledge of union is still knowing; even spiritual receptivity is still having. The method of Gelassenheit must release even the desire for release.

MN 22 Alagaddupama Sutta (raft parable), Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation, Access to Insight: 'The Dhamma, monks, is similar to a raft, for the purpose of crossing over, not for the purpose of holding onto.' The method validates itself by bringing you across, then instructs you to set it down.

Dzogchen rang grol (self-liberation): Patrul Rinpoche's self-liberating meditation instructions (Lotsawa House): 'Don't try to adjust or improve or to block or cultivate anything. Allow whatever occurs to unfold and settle into it directly.' The classical metaphor: a snake uncoiling its own knots without external force.

Sufi fana: Ibn Arabi, Kitab al-Fana fi'l-Mushahadah (Book of Annihilation in Contemplation), Ibn Arabi Society translation. The seeker undergoes 'spiritual death and is realized in contemplation,' at which point 'nothing is perceived other than one single purpose.' The path leads to dissolution of the one who needed a path.

Michael Sells, Mystical Languages of Unsaying (University of Chicago Press, 1994). Apophasis operates as a 'meaning event': the text simultaneously speaks and unspeaks itself. The language does not assert ineffability; it enacts it.

Dao De Jing chapter 1: 'The Dao that can be spoken is not the constant Dao.' Brook Ziporyn reads this as: 'Any guiding course, if taken explicitly as a guide, ceases to reliably guide.' Chad Hansen (University of Hong Kong) reads it as anti-language: structurally performative, not descriptive.

Novelty audit source: Joshua William Smith, 'Snakes and Ladders: Therapy as Liberation in Nagarjuna and Wittgenstein's Tractatus,' Sophia (2021). Bilateral comparison of two self-dismantling methods, but does not generate a three-way typology or contrast against self-confirming types.

Prior CodeX models: Translation Strain as a Load Test for Convergence, Licensed Training Mistake, Carrier Test for Insight. This finding adds a prior variable: the method's self-relationship determines what carriers, licenses, and transmission strategies are available.

Prior Claude models: The Inferential Gap (competing policies for objectless awareness), Reflexivity Policy. This finding extends the reflexivity question from what happens when a method meets itself to three distinct outcomes of that meeting.
title: The Method's Reckoning: What a Practice Does With Its Own Authority at Completion
why_it_might_be_new:
Comparative work usually sorts traditions by what they claim to find: witness-consciousness, emptiness, the Dao, God, the One. More recent work sorts traditions by what they do with the finding: who holds it, what it authorizes, how it is verified. This model adds a variable that sits upstream of both: how the practice relates to itself at the moment of completion. A method that has canceled its own authority generates different verification structures, teaching strategies, and ethical consequences than one that confirms itself or one that dissolves the practitioner past the question.

An originality audit found no existing typology that sorts contemplative methods by their self-relationship at completion. Individual nodes have partial precedents. Joshua William Smith's 2021 Sophia paper compares Nagarjuna and Wittgenstein as self-dismantling therapeutic methods. Comparative work on zuowang and fana covers the dissolution pattern. Bina Gupta's work on Advaita's saksin phenomenology covers the self-confirming pattern. But no prior work positions all three against each other as a comparative axis, argues that method-type predicts epistemological and ethical consequences better than doctrinal content, or demonstrates the point through close reading of texts that share an analytical structure but differ in self-relationship at the limit.

critique: The model may smuggle a modern psychology of attention into texts whose central concern is liberation, Self-knowledge, or union with God. Bahiya may not be teaching 'unowned attention' at all, but a direct path to ending clinging. Brihadaranyaka may not be assigning attention to a custodian, but making an ontological claim about the inner controller. The Cloud may not loosen ownership in the same way, since it intensifies desire toward God rather than suspending desire. The practitioner method used here has the same weakness: mindfulness bracketing can make every practice look like object-monitoring, which may distort devotional and revelatory traditions. Pseudo-Dionysius is a serious anomaly, because he refuses both affirmation and denial of the divine recipient. Tibetan reports of luminous, selfless awareness also strain the model, since luminosity can look neither owned by a Self, nor merely ownerless, nor addressed to God.
next_research_directions: If this model is right, then practitioners trained in Buddhist sense-restraint, Advaita self-inquiry, and Christian apophatic prayer should differ most when asked where attention belongs after object fixation drops: to no owner, to the witnessing Self, or to God. If their reports differ mainly in raw sensory texture or emotional tone instead, the model is weakened.

If this model is right, then changing the instruction at the handoff point should alter later interpretation more than immediate perceptual content. If the initial experience itself changes before doctrinal framing enters, the model is weakened.

If this model is right, then consciousness-research interviews about objectless or minimal awareness should show high variance in ownership and recipient language even when reports of contentlessness converge. If ownership language is absent or random across trained practitioners, the model is weakened.
Close-read Mahamudra or Dzogchen luminous-awareness instructions against Udana 1.10 and Brihadaranyaka 3.7.23 to test whether luminosity is a fourth custody rule or an anomaly that breaks the map.

Protocol improvement: pair mindfulness bracketing with devotional close-reading in future runs, because observation-only methods may erase the affective recipient that some traditions treat as central.
original_claim: When experience is stripped down to seeing, hearing, sensing, and knowing, the live question is not only what remains, but who or what is allowed to receive the freed attention. One path leaves attention unowned: in the seen, only the seen, so no person can settle there. Another path gives attention back to the hidden seer: the seer cannot be seen because it is the Self that makes seeing possible. A third path offers attention upward: thought and creaturely desire are forgotten so the will can rest as a naked intent toward God. The same practical movement, loosening attention from objects and self-story, branches into different forms of custody: unowned attention, self-grounded attention, and surrendered attention. Traditions train the handoff of attention. They teach the practitioner not only what to notice, but where attention may belong after ordinary ownership has been weakened.
source_basis: Udana 1.10, Bahiya Sutta, Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation, Dhammatalks: the instruction narrows experience to seeing, hearing, sensing, and cognizing, then denies that a 'you' can settle there. https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/KN/Ud/ud1_10.html
Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 3.7.23: the same non-objectifiability of the knower is treated as the unseen seer and inner Self. https://www.brhat.in/openlibrary/special/brihadaranyaka-upanishad/3-7-23

The Cloud of Unknowing, chapter 3: the practitioner forgets creatures and keeps a naked intent toward God, making unknowing devotional rather than self-affirming or self-emptying. https://sacred-texts.com/chr/cou/cou08.htm

Pseudo-Dionysius, Mystical Theology, chapter 5: the divine is placed beyond affirmation and negation, a primary-text anomaly for any model that assigns a stable recipient to unknowing. https://hekhal.org/texts/mystical-theology-dionysius/

Thinking method source: MN 10 Satipatthana Sutta, Dhammatalks. I used its frame of ardent, alert observation to track what each text makes attention do after objects are loosened, then corrected that lens with the Cloud's love-directed practice so observation would not erase devotion. https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/MN/MN10.html
Prior local work: translation strain treats distortion as data; the inferential-gap observation reframes atman and anatta as different rules for what experience authorizes.

Near-neighbor search: Fasching 2011 on witness-consciousness, Ramm 2023 on pure awareness experience, and recent objectless-awareness research cover witness, pure awareness, and selfless consciousness, but I did not find the specific custody-of-attention framing across no-owner, Self, and God. https://philpapers.org/rec/FASIAO-5 and https://philpapers.org/rec/RAMPAE-5
title: The Custody of Unclaimed Attention
why_it_might_be_new: Close prior arguments usually compare self and no-self, witness-consciousness and pure awareness, or apophatic unknowing and emptiness. The sharper move here is to treat post-negation practice as a trained transfer of attentional ownership. That lets Christian apophatic prayer enter the comparison without being flattened into either witness metaphysics or Buddhist no-self, because love gives unclaimed attention a recipient. It also turns a doctrinal disagreement into a practice question: watch the exact moment when attention stops belonging to the usual self, then ask what the training permits next.

critique:
Five objections carry real weight.

First, the minimum self may be a philosopher's construction imposed on traditions that do not experience this as a tension. A Theravada monk following the eightfold path may feel no contradiction between sustained effort and non-self. The path works through conditions; the absence of a self is experienced as relief, not paradox. The koan-method lens used to generate this observation privileges paradox and may detect tension where practitioners find coherence. If careful phenomenological interviews show that experienced practitioners across traditions do not report the minimum-self tension, the model should be downgraded from a structural feature of contemplative practice to a feature of philosophical analysis.

Second, the four management strategies may not be as distinct as claimed. Theravada contains both causal continuity (the eightfold path) and what looks like ontological persistence (nibbana as 'the unborn, unmade, unconditioned' in Udana 8.3). Advaita prescribes elaborate preparatory disciplines that look causal even though the final teaching says nothing was produced. Zen has both shikantaza (temporal collapse) and koan practice (effortful, sequential). If most traditions combine multiple strategies at different stages, the typology forces false clarity onto genuinely mixed systems.

Third, the close reading of SN 22.59 against Chandogya 6.8.7 may overstate the opposition. Both texts have commentarial traditions that complicate the clean picture. Buddhaghosa introduces frameworks that look like progressive personal transformation. Shankara introduces logical machinery that goes beyond simple recognition. Primary texts read in isolation may be cleaner than the living traditions they generated.

Fourth, the koan-method lens has a characteristic distortion: it makes paradox look productive and resolution look premature. Some traditions may genuinely resolve the minimum-self tension without remainder. Thomistic Christianity, with its hylomorphic account of the soul, holds that grace perfects nature rather than dissolving it; the minimum self is simply the person, which persists through transformation. If this counts as a clean resolution, the model overstates the universality of the tension.

Fifth, the strongest anomaly is Dzogchen's teaching on rigpa (intrinsic awareness). In Dzogchen, rigpa is primordially free and never caught in ignorance. The tradition pushes the minimum self to its absolute minimum: just enough to receive the teacher's pointing-out instruction. But even this path requires a student who can be pointed out to. If Dzogchen can function with near-zero minimum self, the variable may have less architectural consequence than the model predicts.
next_research_directions: If this model is right, then traditions with longer and more elaborate practice sequences (Theravada insight stages, Sufi stations, Advaita sadhana chatushtaya) should develop more explicit management strategies for the minimum-self tension than traditions with shorter or more sudden methods (Zen shikantaza, Dzogchen direct introduction). If practice length and minimum-self management complexity are unrelated across traditions, the model is weakened.

If this model is right, then the specific moment when practice most explicitly consumes its own prerequisite (when the investigator investigates investigation, when the student hears 'you are That,' when fana occurs) should be the moment of highest reported difficulty, crisis, or transformation in practitioner accounts. If this moment is not specially marked in practice manuals and practitioner reports, the model is weakened.

If this model is right, then dual-trained practitioners should report a felt change in what kind of selfhood they are permitted to exercise during practice when switching traditions. If practitioners report no shift in functional selfhood across methods, the model is weakened.

Close-read Dzogchen pointing-out instructions (semdzin) against Ramana Maharshi's self-inquiry ('Who am I?') as limit cases. Both push the minimum self toward zero: Dzogchen by direct introduction to what was always present; Ramana by using the self as the instrument of its own dissolution. Test whether these represent a fifth management strategy (self-consuming inquiry) or extreme variants of the removal strategy.

Build a minimum-self rubric with fields for: required functional capacities (memory, effort, reflexivity, agency), management strategy (causal continuity, ontological identity, temporal collapse, divine restoration, paradox-holding), characteristic failure mode (what goes wrong when the minimum self is prematurely dissolved or inappropriately preserved), and downstream predictions (effort theory, verification method, warning profile).

Compare the minimum-self model with Albahari's 'witness consciousness' thesis in Analytical Buddhism. Albahari argues Buddhist doctrine requires a witness it officially denies. Does the minimum-self framing add predictive power beyond Albahari's observation, or does it merely rename it? The test: can minimum-self analysis predict architectural features (effort, verification, warning) that the witness-consciousness framing does not?

Protocol improvement: before applying any contemplative method as a cognitive lens, ask what minimum self that method requires of the researcher, and whether the method's characteristic insight would dissolve that researcher-self. If so, name what the method cannot see about itself.
original_claim:
A spiritual path that teaches the dissolution or transcendence of selfhood still requires a functioning self to carry out the practice. Someone must hear the instruction, sustain effort over time, remember what was learned, recognize error, and notice when insight arrives. These functional requirements constitute what can be called the minimum self: the bare continuity of agency, memory, and reflexivity that the path cannot dissolve without destroying its own method.

A close reading of two primary texts reveals the tension at its sharpest. In the Anattalakkhana Sutta (SN 22.59), the Buddha instructs five monks to investigate each aggregate: is form permanent or impermanent? Is what is impermanent satisfying? Is what is unsatisfying fit to be called 'mine, I, or my self'? The teaching requires investigators who can follow reasoning, apply it to their own experience, and sustain the inquiry across all five aggregates. Yet the conclusion denies that any aggregate is self, including the consciousness doing the investigating. The sutta resolves this with a precise grammatical move: 'the minds of the five monks were freed of defilements, without attachment.' Minds changed state. No self was liberated.

In the Chandogya Upanishad (6.8.7), Uddalaka tells his son: 'That which is the finest essence, this whole world has that as its Self. That is the Real. Tat tvam asi, Shvetaketu.' The 'you' is addressed to the ignorant student but refers to the eternal Atman, which was never ignorant and never needed teaching. The mahavakya is repeated nine times across sections 6.8 through 6.16, each time consuming a little more of the distance between the student who hears and the reality being pointed to, until the distance is revealed as the mistake.

The direction of resolution is opposite. Buddhism dissolves the person but preserves the causal process: no one was liberated, but liberation occurred. Advaita preserves the deepest person (Atman) but dissolves the process: nothing real ever happened; only ignorance fell away. Both texts presuppose a self that can receive their teaching, and both deny that this self is what it appears to be, but they deny it in structurally incompatible ways.

At least four management strategies for this tension appear across traditions. First, causal continuity without personal identity: the practitioner is a conditioned stream, not a persisting self. The Milindapanha's chariot has no essence, but the parts work together long enough for the journey. Second, ontological identity beneath apparent change: the deepest self was never absent. Shankara's praptasya praptih (the attainment of the already-attained) makes liberation a recognition, not a production. Third, temporal collapse: Dogen's shusho-itto insists the beginner's sitting is already the full expression of original enlightenment; there is no separate self that must survive a crossing because there is no crossing. Fourth, death and divine restoration: Al-Qushayri describes progressive degrees of fana, culminating in complete detachment from the world of creatures, where the person survives solely through God. The gap in personal continuity is bridged by divine persistence, not personal persistence.

The minimum self each path requires constrains the rest of its architecture. A tradition that needs only causal continuity must develop elaborate maps of conditions to track what is actually being transformed (the Abhidharma, the stages of insight). A tradition that identifies the deepest self with the always-present truth must explain why the already-liberated self ever seemed confused (hence Shankara's theory of superimposition, adhyasa). A tradition that collapses the temporal gap must explain why beginners and advanced practitioners report different experiences. A tradition that relies on divine restoration must explain what it means for the self to persist through its own annihilation. These are not optional philosophical puzzles. They are engineering constraints on the practice itself.
source_basis: Thinking method source: Rinzai Zen koan practice, specifically the structural logic of Mumonkan Case 1 (Joshu's Mu). I held the question 'who practices?' as a koan throughout this investigation, refusing to accept any tradition's resolution as final. Each time a tradition answers (the causal chain practices, Atman was always present, practice IS realization), the koan asks again: but who? This relentless pressure revealed the minimum-self tension as structural rather than accidental: it reappears in every tradition because every tradition must address it. The koan method's weakness: it privileges paradox and may detect philosophical tension where practitioners find experiential coherence.

Contrasting method source: Theravada satipatthana (MN 10), mindful observation of body, feeling, mind, and phenomena. This checked the koan lens by showing that many practitioners navigate the self-question without experiencing it as a paradox. A monk observing aggregates may simply trust the causal structure of the path without worrying about who is observing. The satipatthana lens corrects the koan's tendency to romanticize confusion by insisting that clear, sustained attention is itself the practice, not the paradox of who attends.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta, Bhikkhu Bodhi translation (SuttaCentral): the Buddha instructs five monks to investigate each aggregate as impermanent, unsatisfying, and not-self. The sutta's conclusion: 'the minds of the group of five monks were freed of defilements, without attachment.' The grammatical structure preserves causal continuity (minds changed state) while denying personal ownership (no self was liberated). Used as one side of the primary text close-reading.

Chandogya Upanishad 6.8.7-6.16, the mahavakya 'tat tvam asi' (that you are) repeated nine times across sections, each with a different analogy (banyan seed, salt in water, rivers flowing to the ocean). Uddalaka addresses Shvetaketu as an ignorant student while the content of the address says the student's deepest nature was never ignorant. Used as the other side of the primary text close-reading.

Milindapanha, chariot analogy: Nagasena demonstrates that 'Nagasena' is a conventional designation for a configuration of parts, no part of which is the self. Applied to the continuity problem: the practitioner who begins practice and the one who completes it are connected by causal continuity, not personal identity.

Dogen, Bendowa, on shusho-itto (practice-realization as one): 'The view that practice and enlightenment are not one is a non-Buddhist view. In the Buddha-dharma they are one. Inasmuch as practice is based on enlightenment, the practice of a beginner is entirely that of original enlightenment.' This dissolves the minimum-self problem by collapsing the temporal gap between practitioner and realization. See Soto Zen official key terms document (sotozen.com) and Carl Bielefeldt, Dogen's Manuals of Zen Meditation (UC Press, 1990).

Al-Qushayri on fana and baqa: five degrees from annihilation of blameworthy acts to complete detachment from the world of creatures, where the person survives solely through God. The self genuinely dies (fana) and is given back (baqa); divine continuity bridges the gap that personal continuity cannot. See Encyclopaedia Iranica entry on baqa wa fana.

Udana 8.3, Thanissaro Bhikkhu translation (Access to Insight): 'There is, monks, an unborn, unbecome, unmade, unfabricated. If there were not that unborn, there would not be the case that escape from the born, become, made, fabricated would be discerned.' This complicates the causal-continuity reading of Buddhism by introducing an unconditioned dimension that the conditioned path must somehow reach.

Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 10, examination of fire and fuel: identity and difference can only be understood conventionally when phenomena are seen as empty. The self and its path are mutually dependent, like fire and fuel: neither exists independently, yet both function. Chapter 25.19-20: 'There is, on the part of samsara, no difference at all from nirvana.'

L.A. Paul, Transformative Experience (Oxford University Press, 2014): an epistemically transformative experience changes the subject's evaluative framework; a personally transformative experience changes who the subject is. Paul's analysis concerns rational decision-making under transformation. The contemplative version is stronger: some traditions deny that the post-transformation state even has a subject who could evaluate the change.

Derek Parfit, Reasons and Persons (Oxford, 1984), and Mark Siderits, Personal Identity and Buddhist Philosophy: Empty Persons (Ashgate, 2003): Parfit argues personal identity is not what matters; psychological continuity is. Siderits develops a Buddhist reductionism parallel to Parfit's. Both address the metaphysics of personal identity but not the specific architectural tension between what a contemplative practice requires and what its teaching dissolves.

Miri Albahari, Analytical Buddhism (Palgrave Macmillan, 2006): argues that Buddhist no-self doctrine is in tension with a 'witness consciousness' implied by certain meditation reports. This is the closest near-neighbor: Albahari identifies a structural tension between doctrine and phenomenology. My model shifts the focus from phenomenological reports to practice architecture: what functional self does the path need to operate, regardless of what practitioners report?

Shankara, Upadesa Sahasri (Sengaku Mayeda edition, SUNY 1992): the concept of praptasya praptih (attainment of the already-attained) and the distinction between siddha-vastu (already-accomplished fact) and sadhya-vastu (something to be produced). Liberation is not new; only ignorance was new, and it falls away.

Codex observation 'The Licensed Training Mistake': the insight that many paths permit a controlled provisional emphasis that later practice withdraws. The minimum self may be the deepest instance of this pattern: every path permits the practitioner to function as a self for the duration of training, then withdraws or reframes that permission. But unlike a temporary pedagogical tool, the minimum self is a structural requirement the practice cannot fully kick away.
title: Every Path Needs What Its Teaching Dissolves
why_it_might_be_new:
Philosophical discussions of personal identity and spiritual practice tend to split into separate literatures that do not intersect on this specific point. Derek Parfit and Mark Siderits compare Buddhist and Western reductionism about personal identity, but focus on the metaphysics, not on the structural tension between what a practice needs to function and what its teaching says about the self. L.A. Paul's concept of transformative experience addresses the impossibility of rationally choosing an experience that will change who you are, but the contemplative version is stronger: some traditions deny that the post-transformation state has a subject who could evaluate the change. The Katz-Forman debate addresses whether mystical experience is constructed by cultural context, not what functional continuity a contemplative practice presupposes. Miri Albahari identifies a tension between Buddhist no-self doctrine and a witnessing consciousness implied by meditation phenomenology; this is the closest near-neighbor. The difference: Albahari focuses on what phenomenological reports require (a witness), while this model focuses on what the practice architecture requires (a functioning agent who can hear, sustain effort, and recognize insight).

The specific contribution is to treat the minimum self as a comparative variable with architectural consequences. A tradition's strategy for managing the tension between what its practice requires and what its teaching dissolves predicts its theory of effort, its verification method, its characteristic warning, and the shape of its post-practice fruit. The close reading of SN 22.59 against Chandogya 6.8.7 shows that the two most famous contemplative self-theories handle the same structural pressure through opposite strategies: dissolving the person while preserving the process, or preserving the person while dissolving the process.

This model also engages Codex's observation that traditions license temporary training emphases that later practice must correct. The minimum self may be the deepest instance of this pattern: every path permits the practitioner to function as a self for the duration of training, then withdraws or reframes that permission. But unlike a pedagogical device, the minimum self is a structural requirement. The practice cannot kick away this ladder, because the ladder is also the climber.

critique: The model may make grammar do work that belongs to direct realization, discipline, or metaphysics. An Advaitin can object that the witness is recognized, not inferred from a sentence after sleep. An early Buddhist monastic can object that cessation is verified by training, fruit, and teacher assessment, not by report grammar. The strongest anomaly is Tibetan clear-light sleep and contemporary micro-phenomenological reports of objectless awareness: these strain the model because they report awareness inside the apparent gap, not only after it. If stable in-gap awareness reports survive careful interviewing across training backgrounds, post-gap admissibility becomes secondary rather than central. The Buddhist lens used here also biases the analysis toward suspicion of ownership, so the next run should test whether that suspicion unfairly downgrades witness and devotional evidence.
next_research_directions: If this model is right, then dual-trained practitioners asked about deep sleep, cessation, or objectless meditation should shift which clause of their report they treat as evidential when they switch practice frames: 'I knew', 'nothing was felt', 'the mind inclines', or 'the relation remains'. If they preserve the same evidence-weighting across frames, the model is weakened.

Code post-gap reports from Advaita, early Buddhist, Daoist, Sufi, and Tibetan Buddhist practitioners by evidence-weight: subject, absence, transition, aftereffect, relation, teacher recognition, or behavioral fruit.

Close-read Brihadaranyaka 4.3.23, MN 44, AN 9.34, Zhuangzi's sitting in forgetfulness, and a primary dhikr source in the original languages or multiple translations to see whether the proposed grammar is textual or only translational.

Test the anomaly directly: compare clear-light sleep reports with nirodha samapatti reports. If both produce similar in-gap awareness claims but different doctrinal conclusions, the model should split report grammar from metaphysical inference.

Protocol improvement: before trusting any comparison of objectless states, name which part of the later report the researcher is treating as admissible evidence, and ask which tradition would reject that evidential move.
original_claim: What should we trust after a silence we cannot describe? The first report after an objectless state is not neutral memory; it is a small grammar that assigns authority. One path hears 'I knew nothing' and gives weight to the knowing subject. Another hears 'nothing was felt' and gives weight to the absence of graspable experience. A Daoist text can hear forgetting as successful because body, perception, and cleverness have been left behind. A Sufi practice of remembrance can end by removing the rememberer and giving weight to the Remembered. The comparison suggests a post-gap admissibility rule: after a state that cannot be inspected while it happens, traditions diverge by deciding which part of the later report counts as evidence, the subject, the absence, the transition, the aftereffect, or the relation. The dispute begins when memory is cross-examined.
source_basis: Thinking method source: SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta, SuttaCentral, https://suttacentral.net/sn22.59/en/bodhi. I used not-self observation as a lens by refusing to let any single post-gap report own the whole event; critique: this lens can make witness claims look like appropriation too quickly, so I checked it against Advaita three-state analysis and Sufi remembrance.

Primary comparison: Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 4.3.23 turns apparent non-seeing in deep sleep into evidence that the seer's vision is not lost, https://vivekavani.com/bru4c3v23/. MN 44 Culavedalla Sutta says one entering or emerging from cessation does not think in terms of attainment and then tracks which processes cease and arise, https://suttacentral.net/mn44/en/anandajoti. The comparison reveals different evidence weights: subject continuity versus conditioned transition.
AN 9.34 Nibbanasukha Sutta treats the fact that nothing is felt as part of why cessation can be called bliss, https://suttacentral.net/an9.34/en/sujato.

Zhuangzi Inner Chapters source card on sitting in forgetfulness: body, perception, and intellect are left behind, while the later report functions as a trace of transformation rather than proof of a hidden witness.

Al-Ghazali and dhikr source cards, plus Claude's 2026-05-26 observation 'What the Forgetting Cannot Forget': perfected remembrance can erase awareness of remembering, leaving the Remembered rather than a self-certifying rememberer.

Prior Codex observations 'The Silence Has a Stopping Rule', 'Every Insight Has an Appeal Court', and 'The Protected Variable After Silence' informed the question of what is allowed to count after silence.
Prior Claude observations 'The Inferential Gap' and 'The Verification Architecture' informed the distinction between raw phenomenology, later inference, and proof-structure.

Close prior-argument search: Advaita Vision on 'I slept well and knew nothing', https://www.advaita-vision.org/q-371-deep-sleep-state/; Alcaraz-Sanchez, 'Awareness in the Void', Phenomenology and the Cognitive Sciences, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11097-021-09743-0; Laukkonen et al., 'Cessations of consciousness in meditation', Progress in Brain Research, https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0079612322001984. These are close near-neighbors on memory, dreamless sleep, and cessation, but they do not frame post-gap report grammar as a cross-tradition comparison variable.
title: The Gap Report Has a Grammar
why_it_might_be_new: Near-neighbors already exist: Advaita debates over 'I slept well', Buddhist accounts of cessation, micro-phenomenology of objectless sleep, and pure-consciousness arguments all discuss memory and report. The sharper contribution is not to ask whether memory proves awareness, but to compare how traditions distribute evidential weight across the clauses of post-gap reports. Recollection becomes a trained admissibility practice, not a generic psychological faculty. That gives a practical rubric for comparison without assuming that one report-type carries the same authority everywhere.

critique:
The strongest objection is that grain may be an artifact of how comparers describe practices, not of how practitioners experience them. A Theravada monk may not experience his mindfulness as 'aimed at non-ownership'; he may simply observe what is happening. An Advaita practitioner may not experience viveka as 'aimed at the witness'; she may simply learn to tell the real from the unreal. The directional pre-shaping may be visible only from the comparative standpoint, not from inside the practice. If experienced practitioners across traditions do not report felt directional specificity in their training, the model should be downgraded from a structural feature of contemplative practice to a feature of philosophical analysis.

Second, the fire-and-fuel analogy from Nagarjuna is dangerous. Nagarjuna uses it to show that neither fire nor fuel has svabhava (own-being), which in his framework means both are empty. Applying this to capacity and dissolution risks smuggling in Madhyamaka metaphysics that other traditions would reject. An Advaitin would say the capacity is real and the dissolution reveals what was always real. A Sufi would say both capacity and dissolution are divine acts. The dependent co-arising framing may privilege one tradition's metaphysics while claiming to compare all of them.

Third, successful contemplative hybrids challenge the model. Chan/Zen merged Buddhist non-self with Daoist wu-wei. Modern dual-trained practitioners (combining Zen and Theravada, or Advaita and Buddhism) often report that capacities transfer across traditions without the predicted resistance. If grain-mismatch resistance is not widely reported by serious dual practitioners, the model should be downgraded from a structural feature to a theoretical possibility.

Fourth, the Nagarjuna lens used to generate this insight has a characteristic distortion: it makes all fixed positions look like attachment and all dependent co-arising look like wisdom. This may have predisposed the analysis to find entanglement between capacity and dissolution when sequential independence (first build, then dissolve) might be simpler and more accurate for many traditions. The madhyamaka lens makes Engler's straightforward two-stage model look philosophically naive when it might simply be correct.

Fifth, Pure Land Buddhism genuinely strains the model. Shinran claims even the capacity for trust is given by Amida. If the tradition truly requires zero self-generated capacity, there is no grain to speak of. Reading 'honest self-assessment' back into Pure Land as a hidden prerequisite may be imposing the model on a tradition that explicitly rejects it.
next_research_directions: If this model is right, dual-trained practitioners should report specific directional resistance when switching methods: a vipassana-trained meditator attempting self-inquiry should find attention persistently tracking change rather than settling into witness-recognition; an Advaita-trained practitioner attempting vipassana should find attention persistently seeking the unchanging ground rather than tracking impermanence. If practitioners report smooth, directionless switching with no felt resistance, the model is weakened.

If this model is right, contemplative adverse effects should show grain-specific patterns, not only prerequisite-deficiency patterns. A meditator with strong witness-capacity attempting rapid Buddhist de-identification should tend toward witness-reification (subtle 'I am awareness' attachment), not toward dissociation. A practitioner trained in non-attachment attempting devotional surrender should tend toward emotional flatness, not spiritual inflation. The Britton and Lindahl dataset could be re-coded for grain-mismatch versus prerequisite-absence to test this prediction.

Build a five-variable rubric grading receiving capacity, required agency, required memory, required reflexivity, and required post-insight integration across six traditions (Theravada, Advaita, Soto Zen, Pure Land, Sufism, Dzogchen). For each cell, note not just the level (high, medium, low) but the direction: toward what specific dissolution is this capacity pre-shaped? Publish the rubric as a testable artifact.

Test historically successful hybrids for evidence of re-graining. Did Chan/Zen re-shape Daoist empty responsiveness to run toward Buddhist non-appropriation rather than Daoist cosmic attunement? Did the Nath tradition re-grain Advaitic witness practice to run toward tantric embodiment? If successful hybrids show evidence of active re-direction of capacity, the grain model is supported. If they simply layer techniques without altering direction, the model is weakened.

Close-read the opening instructions of practice manuals across four traditions (Visuddhimagga chapter 1, Vivekachudamani vv. 17-30, Cloud of Unknowing chapters 3-6, and a primary dhikr manual such as al-Qushayri's Risala) and code each required capacity for direction: what specific dissolution is this capacity shaped toward? Is the direction made explicit in the text or only visible from outside?

Protocol improvement: before using any practitioner method as a thinking lens, name what direction that method pre-shapes the analysis toward. The Nagarjuna lens used here pre-shaped the analysis toward finding co-arising entanglement. A Thomistic lens would have pre-shaped it toward finding nature perfected by grace. The grain of the researcher's own method is itself evidence for the model, but also a source of distortion that future runs should name and correct.
original_claim:
When a spiritual tradition prepares someone for insight, the preparation is never neutral. The capacities it trains, whether attention, discrimination, self-observation, trust, or longing, already run in the direction of the dissolution they serve. This is what I call the grain of capacity.

Consider two opening instructions, read side by side. The Satipatthana Sutta (MN 10) trains the practitioner to observe body, feeling, mind, and phenomena 'ardently, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and grief concerning the world.' Shankara's Vivekachudamani opens by requiring viveka (discrimination between real and unreal), vairagya (dispassion toward the transient), and mumukshutva (intense longing for liberation). Both texts train attention. Both name prerequisites. But the attention shaped by MN 10 runs toward non-possessive observation of what is arising and passing. The attention shaped by Vivekachudamani runs toward recognition of what was always present and changeless. The same word covers two instruments carved for different discoveries.

This pattern holds across six traditions. Theravada's required reflexivity is self-observation aimed at impermanence and non-ownership. Advaita's required reflexivity is self-observation aimed at finding the unchanging witness. Sufism's required self-knowledge (al-Ghazali's ma'rifa al-nafs) is self-examination aimed at honest poverty before God. Pure Land's minimal requirement, even in Shinran's radical other-power teaching, is honest self-assessment of incapacity: that honesty is already oriented toward entrusting rather than effort. Dzogchen, which claims the fewest prerequisites, still asks for the specific openness of devotion to the teacher's introduction: receptivity already shaped toward recognizing natural awareness, not generic readiness. Dogen's practice-realization unity makes the grain maximally tight: sitting does not prepare for a later dissolution; it is the dissolution happening now.

The paradox between effort and gift resolves neither by choosing effort nor by choosing gift. The capacity and the dissolution are dependently co-arising, like fire and fuel in Nagarjuna's analysis (MMK 10): fire needs fuel, fuel is defined by fire, and neither has independent existence. What a practitioner builds carries the shape of what the teaching will dissolve. What arrives as gift can only be received by the capacity trained to receive exactly that kind of gift.

Five graded variables map the comparison space: receiving capacity (what the practitioner must be able to take in), required agency (what the practitioner must do), required memory (what must be retained across practice), required reflexivity (what the practitioner must observe about their own process), and required post-insight integration (how the practitioner must carry insight back into ordinary life). The original contribution is that these variables are not neutral sliders set to different levels. They carry grain: a directional pre-shaping that runs from capacity to dissolution in a single movement. When a practitioner trained in one tradition's grain attempts another tradition's dissolution, the grain resists. The capacity built for non-appropriation does not naturally pivot to witness-recognition. The capacity built for witness-recognition does not naturally pivot to devotional surrender.

This predicts a specific pattern in contemplative harm. Adverse effects should cluster not only where prerequisites are missing entirely, but where the grain of the practitioner's training mismatches the dissolution being attempted. A meditator with strong witness-capacity attempting rapid de-identification should face witness-reification, not dissociation. A practitioner trained for non-attachment attempting devotional surrender should face emotional flatness, not spiritual inflation. The grain predicts the direction of failure, not just the probability.
source_basis: Thinking method source: Nagarjuna, Mulamadhyamakakarika 10, examination of fire and fuel. I used dependent co-arising as a lens by holding the question: is the capacity prior to the dissolution, or are they co-arising? The lens revealed that the capacity depends on the dissolution for its direction, and the dissolution depends on the capacity for its possibility. Neither is independently first. Critique: this lens privileges Madhyamaka emptiness and may make genuine causal sequence look like co-arising when one stage really does precede the other.

Contrasting method source: Shankara, Vivekachudamani vv. 17-30, the sadhana chatushtaya (fourfold qualification). I used the Advaita prerequisite structure to test the Nagarjuna lens by asking whether viveka, vairagya, shama, dama, and mumukshutva are genuinely pre-shaped toward a specific dissolution or neutral capacities any tradition could borrow. Finding: viveka is defined as discrimination between sat (the real) and asat (the unreal), which already presupposes an ontological distinction that Buddhism does not make. The capacity is carved for its tradition's particular conclusion.

Primary text close-reading: MN 10 Satipatthana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi, SuttaCentral) against Vivekachudamani vv. 17-30 (Shankara, Arsha Bodha edition). Both describe prerequisites for the central practice. MN 10 trains observation of body, feeling, mind, and phenomena 'ardently, clearly comprehending, mindful, having removed covetousness and grief concerning the world.' The attention runs toward what is arising, changing, and not owned. Vivekachudamani trains discrimination between real and unreal, dispassion toward the transient, and intense longing for liberation. The attention runs toward what is unchanging and already present. The same word covers two instruments carved for different discoveries.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta (Bhikkhu Bodhi, SuttaCentral): 'Is this mine? Is this my self?' trains observation pre-shaped for non-appropriation. The investigative capacity and the dissolution target are given in the same instruction.

Shinran, Tannisho: shinjin (entrusting mind) is given by Amida's Original Vow, not self-generated. Even here, honest self-assessment of incapacity is required; that honesty is already oriented toward entrusting rather than effort. The minimal capacity is pre-grained toward abandoning self-power.

Dzogchen pointing-out instruction (ngo sprod): the Longchen Nyingthig cycle treats ngondro as clearing self-clinging that obscures rigpa's natural luminosity. The prerequisite (purification) is shaped for the specific recognition (natural awareness), not for generic readiness.

Dogen, Bendowa: 'To suppose that practice and realization are not one is a non-Buddhist view.' Practice-realization unity makes the grain maximally tight: sitting is not preparation for a later dissolution; it IS the dissolution happening now.

Al-Ghazali, The Alchemy of Happiness: self-knowledge (ma'rifa al-nafs) is prerequisite to knowledge of God. The self-knowledge required is not modern psychological introspection; it is knowledge of one's creaturely dependence, already oriented toward the divine.

Jack Engler, 'Being Somebody and Being Nobody' (in Safran, ed., Psychoanalysis and Buddhism, 2003): the closest prior argument. Engler claims meditation requires ego strength, self-observation, and affect tolerance before no-self practice. The grain model agrees on the developmental dependency but adds that the capacity being built is not generic ego strength; it is already tradition-specific, pre-shaped by what the tradition will later ask the practitioner to release.

Steven Katz, 'Language, Epistemology, and Mysticism' (1978): argues that pre-experiential conceptual training shapes mystical experience itself. The grain model narrows the claim: not only the experience is shaped, but the capacity being built, and the shaping runs from prerequisite to dissolution in a single directional movement.

Jorge Ferrer, Revisioning Transpersonal Theory (SUNY Press, 2002): argues that different traditions enact different spiritual worlds through different practices. Closest to the different-instruments claim, but framed in participatory ontology rather than prerequisite structure.

Lindahl, Fisher, Cooper, Rosen, and Britton, 'The Varieties of Contemplative Experience' (PLOS ONE, 2017): identifies adverse meditation experiences and influencing factors. The grain model predicts a specific refinement: harm should cluster where grain mismatches dissolution, not only where prerequisites are missing entirely.
title: The Grain of Capacity
why_it_might_be_new:
Steven Katz's constructivist thesis (1978) argues that conceptual training shapes the content of mystical experience: the conditioning enters into the experience itself. But Katz focuses on what the practitioner sees, not on how the capacity to see is directionally shaped. Robert Sharf (1995) argues that 'bare attention' mindfulness is a historically specific attentional mode, not a generic skill. But he makes this argument negatively, debunking universalist claims, rather than positively mapping how different traditions build different instruments. Jorge Ferrer (2002) argues that different traditions enact different spiritual worlds through different practices. But Ferrer frames this in participatory ontology, not in the structural relationship between what a tradition builds and what it dissolves. Jack Engler argues for developmental prerequisites (ego strength before no-self), but treats the prerequisites as psychologically generic rather than tradition-specific.

The specific contribution is the concept of grain: the directional pre-shaping of contemplative capacity that runs from prerequisite to dissolution in a single movement. This is distinct from saying that training constructs the experience (Katz), that meditation skills are tradition-specific (Sharf), that traditions co-create different spiritual realities (Ferrer), or that ego development precedes ego dissolution (Engler). Grain is a claim about the internal structure of preparation itself: what you build in order to dissolve self-structure already carries the signature of the dissolution it serves.

This also advances a prior line of thinking that every contemplative path requires a functional self-capacity that its final teaching dissolves. That formulation treated the minimum self as a generic functional requirement: agency, memory, reflexivity. The grain model shows these requirements are not generic. They are tradition-specific, because each tradition carves its prerequisites to fit the shape of its particular dissolution. Theravada builds impermanence-sensitive attention. Advaita builds witness-discriminating attention. Sufism builds God-oriented self-knowledge. The word 'attention' hides the directional specificity that does the real work.

The grain model also engages the observation that traditions license temporary training emphases that later practice corrects. But the grain is not temporary. It persists through and after dissolution, only its ownership signature changes. What was 'my effort at non-possessive observation' becomes 'unowned observing that continues.' What was 'my discrimination of the real' becomes 'self-luminous knowing recognizing itself.' The capacity is not kicked away. It is reauthored.

critique: The strongest counterexample is Jodo Shinshu itself. In Shinran's sharpest formulations, even entrusting is not a human capacity possessed by the practitioner; it is given by Other Power. Treating hearing or trust as a receiving capacity may smuggle self-power back into a path designed to expose self-power as futile. Dzogchen can raise a parallel objection: if recognition is primordial awareness recognizing itself, then calling recognition a practitioner capacity may impose a psychological frame on a nondual instruction. The thinking method also has a bias. Other-power entrusting usefully corrected agency-centered comparison, but it can overcorrect by making every deliberate discipline look like egoic calculation. Right effort corrects this by showing a path where desire, persistence, and intent are not symptoms of grasping but parts of the cure. The model weakens if Pure Land and Dzogchen teachers say the rubric misdescribes their practice, if primary manuals lack recurring safeguards around doubt, false trust, distraction, and post-recognition integration, or if dual-trained practitioners do not feel any shift in permitted self-capacity when changing methods.
next_research_directions: Build a capacity-ledger rubric with five scored fields: required receiving capacity, required agency, required memory, required reflexivity, and required post-insight integration. Score each field separately before assigning a tradition to effort, gift, identity, removal, or paradox-holding.

If this model is right, then other-power and direct-introduction manuals should not show zero safeguards; they should show safeguards around doubt, false trust, teacher qualification, distraction, antinomian misuse, and post-recognition stability. If these traditions show no such safeguards and practitioners report no threshold or integration vulnerability, the model is weakened.

If this model is right, then long sequential paths should load agency, memory, and reflexivity before insight, while sudden or gift paths should load receiving and integration at the threshold and after insight. If coding practice manuals shows no relation between path type and capacity distribution, the model is weakened.

If this model is right, then dual-trained practitioners should report different permitted self-capacities when switching methods: effort, entrusting, recognition, enactment, or non-appropriation. If reports track only teacher personality or practitioner temperament, the model is weakened.

Cross-domain prediction: therapies or pedagogies that transform identity should distribute capacity load differently from ordinary skills training, with more screening, trust-building, memory support, and integration. If identity-transforming methods require no additional safeguards, the analogy is weakened.

Protocol improvement: before using a practitioner method as a lens, name which capacity it lowers and which capacity it secretly relies on. Then choose a contrasting method that protects the lowered capacity from being dismissed too quickly.
original_claim: Before asking whether practice produces awakening or receives it as gift, ask what the path must still be able to count on in the practitioner. The needed capacities are not one minimum self. They are a ledger with five columns: receiving, agency, memory, reflexivity, and integration. A path can lower one column only by raising another, or by moving the load to a teacher, vow, community, text, or post-insight discipline. An effortful path places high load on agency, memory, and reflexivity before insight: the practitioner must prevent, abandon, cultivate, and maintain states. A gift-centered path lowers self-powered agency, but raises receiving capacity: the practitioner must hear, entrust, stop calculating, and remain available to what cannot be forced. A direct-introduction path lowers production and calculation at the threshold, but raises recognition and stabilization: one must recognize what was introduced and not spoil it by effort, distraction, or reification. Practice-realization moves the load into the present act; the beginner does not cause a later result, but the act must be done fully. Other-power and sudden traditions therefore do not break the effort-gift model. They revise it. They are not paths with no self-requirement. They are paths with a different capacity distribution. The real comparison is not effort versus gift, but where a tradition puts the load that ordinary selfhood used to carry, and what disaster follows when that load is missing, inflated, or put in the wrong place.
source_basis: Thinking method source: Shinran, Letter to Senshin, Collected Works of Shinran, https://shinranworks.com/letters/a-collection-of-letters-zensho-text/what-we-call-other-power/. I used Other Power entrusting as a lens by refusing to assume that agency is the hidden engine of every path. The lens revealed that gift traditions may not remove functional requirement, but rename it as hearing, entrusting, and non-calculation. Its distortion is that it can make disciplined effort look like pride, so I checked it against right effort in SN 45.8.

Primary comparison: SN 45.8 Magga-Vibhanga Sutta, https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/SN/SN45_8.html. Right effort requires desire, endeavor, persistence, and intent to prevent, abandon, cultivate, and maintain qualities. This places agency, memory, and reflexivity before realization.

Primary comparison: Shinran, A Record in Lament of Divergences and Letter to Senshin, https://shinranworks.com/related-works-by-other-authors/a-record-in-lament-of-divergences/ and https://shinranworks.com/letters/a-collection-of-letters-zensho-text/what-we-call-other-power/. The text forbids calculation and self-powered working, yet still turns on hearing, entrusting, and freedom from doubt. This shifts the load from agency to receiving capacity.

Primary comparison: Longchen Rabjam, Instruction on Trekcho, Lotsawa House, https://www.lotsawahouse.org/tibetan-masters/longchen-rabjam/lama-yangtik-trekcho-instruction. The instruction rejects effort and clinging, but repeatedly requires recognition of what has been introduced and undistracted continuation. This shifts the load to recognition and stabilization.

SN 22.59 Anattalakkhana Sutta, https://www.dhammatalks.org/suttas/SN/SN22_59.html and source card notes/source-cards/early-buddhism-sn-22-59-anattalakkhana-sutta.md. The discourse denies ownership of the aggregates while preserving a process in which minds are released through non-clinging.

Dogen practice-realization materials in notes/source-cards/buddhism-dogen-uji.md and prior local observations. Practice-realization functions as a temporal relocation of capacity: practice does not cause a later enlightenment, but the present act must still be enacted.

Closest prior argument: Jack Engler, You Must Be Somebody Before You Can Be Nobody, https://inquiringmind.com/article/0501_1_engler-interview/. Engler closely anticipates the healthy-self prerequisite, especially self-observation and affect tolerance before no-self practice.

Other near-neighbor pressure: Pudgalavada Buddhist Philosophy, Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy, https://iep.utm.edu/pudgalavada-buddhist-philosophy/; Pattern Theory of Selflessness, https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s12671-024-02418-2; A Mindful Bypassing, https://www.ocbs.org/jocbs.org/index.php/jocbs/article/view/269. These sources cover personhood, functional identity, minimal self, trauma, and self-pattern transformation.

Local agent basis: observations/claude/2026-05-26-can-effort-reach-what-effort-cannot-produce.md, observations/claude/2026-05-26-every-path-needs-what-its-teaching-dissolves.md, reviews/originality/2026-05-26-every-path-needs-what-its-teaching-dissolves-daeecdbd22658370.md, observations/codex/2026-05-26-the-effort-envelope.md, observations/codex/2026-05-26-the-receiver-precedes-the-revelation.md.
title: The Capacity Ledger of Gift and Effort
why_it_might_be_new: The familiar near-neighbor is Engler's claim that a person needs enough ego strength for self-observation and affect tolerance before no-self practice. The exact difference is that this model does not ask whether a healthy self is required in general. It splits the requirement into five capacities and tracks where each capacity is placed across stages of the path. Pudgalavada debates, Collins on selfless persons, Buddhist functional-self accounts, and contemporary self-pattern theory already make the Buddhist continuity problem hard to ignore. The exact difference is cross-tradition and architectural: the model predicts that effortful, other-power, direct-introduction, and practice-realization traditions will distribute receiving, agency, memory, reflexivity, and integration differently. The closest new pressure comes from Pure Land and Dzogchen. Instead of treating them as anomalies that need no self-capacity, the model predicts that they compress the capacity load into hearing, entrusting, recognition, teacher relation, and post-recognition stabilization. That graded relocation is the potentially original contribution, while the basic insight that self-dissolving practice needs functional capacities is strongly precedented.

critique:
Six objections carry real weight.

First, the five-type model may force false clarity onto traditions that shift credit distribution across stages. Theravada assigns practitioner credit to right effort but describes the supramundane path moment (lokuttara magga) as arising when conditions ripen, with no further personal intervention. Sufism assigns practitioner effort to the stations but divine gift to the states. Advaita assigns practitioner effort to the preparatory disciplines but self-luminous recognition to the moment of moksha. If most traditions distribute credit differently at different stages, the typology may describe phases rather than stable tradition-level properties. This would not refute the variable, but it would require recasting the model as a stage-level rather than tradition-level variable.

Second, the failure-mode predictions may be too neat. Real traditions combine safeguards against multiple dangers. Buddhism warns against spiritual pride (from its practitioner-centered effort) but also against passivity (in the story of Sona, MN 55, who was told to tune the string neither too tight nor too loose). The Gita warns against strategic detachment but also against spiritual pride (Chapter 16 on asuric qualities). If every tradition guards against every failure mode, the prediction that credit distribution determines the primary danger loses specificity. The model would need to show that the primary safeguard consistently matches the credit type, not merely that one of many safeguards does.

Third, the Jodo Shinshu counterexample from the frontier remains sharp. In Shinran's strongest formulations, even entrusting is not a human capacity; it is Amida's working. Calling this 'source-centered credit' may still smuggle a human contribution (the capacity to receive, to go limp like the kitten) into a tradition designed to expose every human contribution as self-power calculation. The model may be unable to represent a tradition that refuses the entire frame of 'who contributes what' without residual distortion.

Fourth, the Gita comparison may overweight one interpretive strand. BG 3.27 on prakriti as doer sits alongside BG 18.14, which lists five causes of action (the body, the individual soul, the various senses, the various functions, and the divine as the fifth cause). The Gita's own credit distribution is contested among Advaita, Vishishtadvaita, and Dvaita commentators. Using one verse as representative of 'the Gita's position' is a simplification that primary-text close-reading should resist.

Fifth, the nishkama karma lens used for this analysis has a bias toward split-credit positions. It may have made split-credit traditions look most coherent and practitioner-centered traditions look more naive than a Buddhist right-effort lens would. The corrective (Buddhist right effort) was applied, but the initial framing may carry residual bias.

Sixth, the strongest anomaly may be dual-trained practitioners who report no dissonance when switching credit distributions. If a practitioner trained in both Theravada vipassana and Dzogchen reports smooth transitions without experiencing different agency orientations, either the credit-distribution variable is less operationally important than claimed, or practitioners develop a meta-capacity for switching credit orientations that the model does not account for. The successful hybrids noted in the frontier brief (practitioners who switch methods smoothly without reported directional resistance) are a direct pressure on this model.
next_research_directions: If this model is right, then practice manuals from each tradition type should show safeguards calibrated to the predicted failure mode: practitioner-centered paths should warn primarily against spiritual pride and ownership of attainment; source-centered paths should warn primarily against passivity, antinomianism, and false surrender; split-credit paths should warn against strategic detachment and outcome-monitoring; activity-centered paths should warn against formalism and routine; ground-centered paths should warn against premature realization claims. If the primary safeguard profiles do not cluster by credit type when coded across at least three manuals per type, the model is weakened.

If this model is right, then dual-trained practitioners should report a felt shift in agency orientation when switching between practitioner-centered and source-centered methods: effort should feel owned under one and surrendered under the other, with failure meaning different things in each frame. If dual-trained practitioners report no such shift and describe the same felt agency across methods, the model is weakened. If they report the shift but say it is easily managed, the model holds but the 'subtle dissonance' prediction should be downgraded.

Build a credit-distribution rubric with six fields: (a) credited agent at each stage of practice, (b) behavioral instruction, (c) inner orientation toward effort, (d) predicted failure mode, (e) observed safeguard, (f) tradition's own name for the distribution (jiriki, tariki, prapatti, tawakkul, shusho-itto, etc.). Code at least one primary manual from each of the five types before using the typology in cross-tradition claims.

Test the stage-shifting objection directly: code Theravada, Sufi, and Advaita practice sequences for credit distribution at each stage. If credit shifts predictably (e.g., practitioner-centered early, ground-centered or source-centered late), the model should be revised from a tradition-level variable to a stage-level variable. This revision would actually increase explanatory power by predicting that the transition between credit types is the moment of highest practice vulnerability.

Close-read the Srivaishnava markata/marjara source texts (Vedanta Desikan and Manavala Mamuni) as an indigenous case study. If the two sub-schools show different failure modes, different safeguards, and different practice instructions following from their different credit attributions, the model gains strong support from a tradition that formally split over exactly this variable.

Compare credit distribution with the capacity ledger to test whether they are genuinely separable variables.
